skill.name: shader-dev
skill.description: Comprehensive GLSL shader techniques for creating stunning visual effects — ray marching, SDF modeling, fluid simulation, particle systems, procedural generation, lighting, post-processing, and more.
skill.summary: Comprehensive GLSL shader techniques for creating stunning visual effects — ray marching, SDF modeli...
skill.author: MiniMax-AI

security_audit.summary: This skill consists entirely of GLSL shader reference documentation (36 markdown files in techniques/ and reference/ directories). Detected risk factors (external_commands, scripts, filesystem, network) are false positives triggered by code examples in markdown documentation. The skill contains no executable scripts, no network requests, no filesystem operations, and no external command invocations. All code samples are GLSL shader snippets for educational/reference purposes. Safe to publish.

content.value_statement: Generate complete, production-ready GLSL shaders for real-time visual effects — from ray-marched 3D scenes to fluid simulations — all running in the browser with WebGL2.
actual_capabilities: Generate complete WebGL2/GLSL shader code for 36+ shader techniques
Create ray-marched scenes with SDF geometry, soft shadows, and ambient occlusion
Build fluid, smoke, and particle simulations using multi-pass rendering
Produce procedural textures and patterns (noise, FBM, Voronoi, fractals)
Implement physically-based rendering (PBR) with multiple lighting models
Generate post-processing effects: bloom, tone mapping, chromatic aberration, glitch
Create volumetric effects: clouds, fog, fire, explosions
Build procedural terrain and ocean rendering with atmospheric scattering
Produce standalone HTML pages with embedded shaders that run in any browser
Adapt ShaderToy GLSL code to WebGL2 standalone pages with proper wrappers
limitations: Shaders run on the GPU and are limited by WebGL2 capabilities — no compute shaders
Performance budget must be respected: ray marching ≤ 128 steps, FBM ≤ 6 octaves, nested loops ≤ 1000 iterations per pixel
Multi-pass effects require framebuffer ping-pong setup (FBOs), which adds complexity
ShaderToy code requires adaptation for WebGL2 (mainImage wrapper, fragColor output, #version directive)
No built-in testing framework — shaders must be visually verified in a browser
Headless rendering environments may have limited GPU power, affecting performance
GLSL has strict type matching and function declaration order requirements

use_cases.title: Interactive Shader Art & Demoscene
use_cases.description: Create mesmerizing visual art pieces, demoscene productions, or generative art installations using GLSL shaders that run in real-time in a web browser. Combine techniques like ray marching, procedural noise, and domain warping to produce unique visuals.
use_cases.target_user: Creative coders, digital artists, demoscene enthusiasts

use_cases.title: WebGL Game Effects & Shaders
use_cases.description: Implement game-ready visual effects including water/ocean surfaces, terrain rendering, particle systems for fire/sparks/snow, post-processing pipelines, and atmospheric lighting — all running client-side in WebGL2.
use_cases.target_user: Web game developers, indie game studios

use_cases.title: Scientific Visualization & Simulation
use_cases.description: Build GPU-accelerated visualizations for scientific data: fluid dynamics simulations, N-body gravity simulations, cellular automata, reaction-diffusion patterns, and volumetric cloud/fog rendering.

prompt_templates.title: Ray-Marched SDF Scene
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a standalone HTML page with a ray-marched SDF scene featuring several geometric objects (spheres, boxes, torus) combined with smooth minimum (smin). Add soft shadows, ambient occlusion, and a three-light outdoor lighting model. Include camera orbit controls via mouse.
prompt_templates.scenario: You need a photorealistic 3D scene generated entirely from math, no 3D models or textures required.

prompt_templates.title: Procedural Landscape
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a WebGL2 shader that renders a procedural landscape. Use heightfield ray marching with FBM terrain (6 octaves with derivatives for erosion-like detail). Add biplanar texturing, height-based atmospheric scattering with Rayleigh/Mie, and Gerstner wave ocean at sea level. Apply ACES tone mapping and 2x SSAA.
prompt_templates.scenario: Create an infinite, procedural terrain that looks realistic without any external assets.

prompt_templates.title: Fluid Simulation with Multi-Pass
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a fluid simulation using a Navier-Stokes solver in GLSL with multi-pass ping-pong framebuffers. Include advection, diffusion, pressure projection (Jacobi iteration), and mouse interaction for adding dye/velocity. Use a color palette to visualize the flow field.
prompt_templates.scenario: Interactive fluid simulation where the user can stir the fluid with their mouse cursor.

prompt_templates.title: Particle System Effects
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a stateless particle system shader for a fire effect. Use procedural noise for turbulence, a warm color palette for the gradient from bright yellow to deep red, and proper alpha blending. Include a second layer of sparks using a separate particle system with gravity.
prompt_templates.scenario: Add a visually impressive fire effect with sparks to a web page or game.

output_examples.input: Create a kaleidoscope pattern using polar UV manipulation with a Voronoi color palette
output: Complete HTML page with WebGL2 canvas
Fragment shader with polar/log-polar coordinate transformation
Voronoi/Worley noise function for cell pattern generation
Cosine color palette mapping for vibrant colors
Kaleidoscope symmetry via angular repetition (e.g., 8-fold symmetry)
Animated time-based rotation for dynamic visual effect

output_examples.input: Build a path-traced global illumination scene with soft shadows and color bleeding
output: Complete path tracer with Monte Carlo sampling
SDF scene definition with multiple objects and materials
Tonemapping and display for converged image

best_practices: Always respect performance budgets: ≤ 128 ray march steps, ≤ 32 volume samples, ≤ 6 FBM octaves, ≤ 1000 nested loop iterations per pixel
Declare functions before use in GLSL — unlike C/C++, GLSL doesn't support forward declarations in all implementations
Use the multipass-buffer technique for effects requiring state persistence across frames (fluid sim, path tracing, particle systems)
Adapt ShaderToy code properly: wrap mainImage() in main(), use gl_FragCoord instead of fragCoord, declare out vec4 fragColor
Prefer const over #define for values — GLSL macros can't use function calls and have limitations
anti_patterns: Exceeding performance budgets — loops over 1000 iterations per pixel will freeze the browser
Using #define macros for computed values — the GLSL preprocessor can't evaluate function calls in macros
Accessing uniforms that may be optimized away by the compiler — always ensure uniforms are used in the fragment output
Nesting ray marching or volume sampling inside another loop — this multiplies complexity and exceeds GPU time limits

faq.question: Can I use ShaderToy shaders directly with this skill?
faq.answer: Not directly — ShaderToy uses a different entry point (mainImage) and has built-in uniforms. The skill includes WebGL2 adaptation rules to convert ShaderToy code: wrap mainImage in main(), use gl_FragCoord, declare fragColor output, and add #version 300 es. The webgl-pitfalls technique covers common conversion errors.

faq.question: What's the difference between techniques/ and reference/ directories?
faq.answer: techniques/ contains concise implementation guides with core principles, code templates, and step-by-step instructions. reference/ contains the same topics with deeper math derivations, extended theory, and advanced patterns. Start with techniques/, then consult reference/ for deeper understanding.

faq.question: How do I combine multiple shader techniques?
faq.answer: Use the Quick Recipes section in SKILL.md for common combinations (e.g., Photorealistic SDF Scene = sdf-3d + ray-marching + lighting-model + shadows + AO + atmospheric-scattering + post-processing). Each technique file is modular — read multiple files and combine their code patterns in a single shader.

faq.question: Why do I need multipass rendering for some effects?
faq.answer: Some effects (fluid simulation, path tracing, particle systems) require reading the previous frame's output. WebGL2 framebuffers with ping-pong (two alternating FBOs) allow you to render to a texture, then read that texture in the next frame. The multipass-buffer technique explains the setup.

faq.question: Will these shaders work on mobile devices?
faq.answer: WebGL2 is supported on most modern mobile browsers, but mobile GPUs have lower performance budgets. Reduce ray march steps (≤ 64 instead of 128), FBM octaves (≤ 4), and avoid heavy nested loops. Test on target devices.

faq.question: How do I debug GLSL shader errors?
faq.answer: Use the visual debugging techniques from SKILL.md: output normals as colors (nor * 0.5 + 0.5), visualize ray march step counts, check UV coordinates, and isolate lighting contributions. The webgl-pitfalls technique covers common GLSL compilation errors like function signature mismatch and reserved word usage.
